title: GUIDELINES FOR THE SIMPLE EVALUATION OF DIETARY EXPOSURE TO FOOD ADDITIVES

sections.section: GUIDELINES FOR THE SIMPLE EVALUATION OF DIETARY EXPOSURE TO FOOD ADDITIVES
sections.summary: Document title and identification: 'Guidelines for the Simple Evaluation of Dietary Exposure to Food Additives' (CAC/GL 3-1989), adopted 1989 and revised 2014, with former title noted.

States that food additives must have a technological benefit, pose no appreciable health risk, not mislead consumers, and be used at the lowest level necessary per GMP; describes Codex risk analysis (risk assessment, management, communication), JECFA's role in scientific risk assessment and ADI setting, and use of ADI in comparing estimated exposures.

Defines dietary exposure assessment as combining food consumption and additive concentration (exposure = sum of concentration × consumption divided by body weight) and recommends a stepwise approach using conservative screening methods to identify low-risk additives quickly and, where concerns are indicated, progressively more refined assessments (notably TMDI and EDI).
Defines Estimated Daily Intake (EDI) as the amount of an additive ingested by the average consumer based on actual industry use levels or, under GMP, an approximation close to actual use levels.

Advises first identifying and collecting available national data on additive concentrations, food consumption and body weights, recommending use of national data where possible and GSFA or national MLs when needed, and notes the importance of detailed metadata and distinctions between pre- and post-regulation data sources.

sections.section: 3.1 Concentration of the food additives in food
sections.summary: Explains that concentration data needs depend on assessment objective: pre-regulation data should come from manufacturers, post-regulation assessments may use national MLs (or GSFA MLs, which tend to overestimate) and market/analytical monitoring data, and that methodological details (sampling, analytical methods, LOD/LOQ, QA) should be documented.

sections.section: 3.1.1 Regulation of use of food additives
sections.summary: Describes three regulatory situations for additives—(a) authorization by specific use with a positive list and MLs, (b) authorization in specified foods under GMP requiring numerical use levels or industry-provided actual levels, and (c) authorization under GMP in all foods with specific provisions requiring extensive sampling and industry collaboration—and notes complications when processed foods are largely imported.

Describes food consumption data sources (individual dietary surveys, household-level data, national food balance sheets), their characteristics and limitations, advises considering subgroup variation and high consumers using higher percentiles, and suggests a simple correction factor (×3) to approximate high-consumer intake for one dominant food category.

sections.section: 4.1 Criteria for prioritization of evaluation of dietary exposure to food additives
sections.summary: Provides prioritization criteria for exposure assessment: low priority for additives with an ADI of 'not specified' used per GMP, and higher priority for additives with low numerical ADIs used at high levels, authorized in widely consumed foods or consumed by at-risk subgroups, or assigned a numerical ADI and used under GMP.

sections.section: 4.2 Proposed method for a simple evaluation of the dietary exposure to food additives
sections.summary: Proposes a two-stage stepwise method: A) calculate the TMDI by listing permitted foods, applying MLs (or actual/proposed use levels) and average consumption (per capita or 'eaters'); if TMDI < ADI no further work is needed, otherwise B) refine using EDI by narrowing foods to those actually containing the additive and using actual use or analytical levels, then compare EDI to ADI and, if >ADI, consider further refinement and discussions with industry about MLs.

sections.section: B.3 Introduce these more refined data (B.1 and B.2) in the TMDI previously calculated (see section A).
sections.summary: Directs that refined data from checking food lists and actual use levels be applied to the previously calculated TMDI and states the decision rule: if EDI < ADI no concern; if EDI > ADI, pursue more refined assessment and possibly review MLs with industry.
Summarizes that the document provides a stepwise approach to estimate additive exposure to determine whether an ADI may be exceeded.

Annex containing worked examples and tables illustrating TMDI, improved TMDI and EDI calculations with food categories, MLs or measured concentrations, consumption values, and stepwise refinements demonstrating when estimated exposures exceed ADIs.

Shows an improved TMDI using 'eaters' consumption for juices and soft drinks, presents calculated improved TMDI (412.13 mg/day) and a high-consumer TMDI (962 mg/day by multiplying the major contributor by 3), and notes these estimates exceed adult and child ADIs requiring further evaluation.

Presents an EDI example using analytical mean concentrations for margarine, juices and soft drinks to compute an EDI (219.71 mg/day) and a high-consumer EDI (513 mg/day), notes the EDI exceeds the children's ADI, and recommends further refinement or industry consultation to review MLs or use patterns.
